items.title: High risk genotypes
items.contentHtml:
High-risk human papillomavirus genotypes

  • Human papillomaviruses 16 and 18 cause approximately 70% of cervical cancers; Human papillomavirus 16 is the most oncogenic, associated with squamous cell carcinomas and adenocarcinomas. of infection with a high-risk genotype is necessary for the development of squamous intraepithelial lesions and invasive cancer; Integration of the viral genome into host DNA inactivates E2 and overexpresses E6 and E7, which degrade p53 and pRb respectively.

items.title: HPV vaccines
items.contentHtml:
Vaccine against human papillomavirus and covered serotypes

  • Bivalent vaccine (Cervarix): covers human papillomavirus 16 and 18; indicated from 9 years of age, 2-dose schedule (0 and 6 months) if <15 years, 3 doses if ≥15 years.
  • Tetravalent vaccine (Gardasil): covers human papillomavirus 6, 11, 16 and 18; prevents genital warts and cancer.
  • Nonavalent vaccine (Gardasil 9): covers human papillomavirus 6, 11, 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 52 and 58; greater coverage against high-risk genotypes.
  • Vaccination recommended ideally before the start of sexual life; in women up to 45 years of age in some contexts, although the benefit is greater in unexposed nulliparas.

items.title: Cervical cytology
items.contentHtml:
Cervical cytology (Papan smear)

  • Screening with cervical cytology (Papan smear) begins at age 25 and is repeated every 3 years if the result is normal; Sensitivity is 50-70% for high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesions.
  • Reporting according to Bethesda 2014 system: atypical squamous cells of undetermined significance (ASC-US), low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (LSIL), high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (HSIL), atypical squamous cells that do not exclude a high-grade lesion (ASC-H).
  • Management of ASC-US: if the high-risk human papillomavirus test is positive, it is referred to colposcopy; If negative, repeat cytology after 3 years.
  • In women > 30 years old, co-testing (cytology + human papillomavirus test) is preferred every 5 years.

items.title: Colposcopy and biopsy
items.contentHtml:
Colposcopy and directed biopsy

  • Colposcopy is the study of choice after an abnormal cytology or positive human papillomavirus test; allows visualization of the transformation zone with 3-5% acetic acid and lugol.
  • Colposcopic findings: acetowhite epithelium, punctate, mosaic and atypical vessels suggest high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion or invasive cancer.
  • Directed biopsy of the most suspicious areas is performed; If the squamocolumnar junction is not completely visible, endocervical curettage is indicated to rule out endocervical disease.
  • Colloposcopy has a sensitivity of 85-90% to detect high-grade lesions when the transformation zone is visible.

items.title: FIGO Classification
items.contentHtml:
FIGO classification of cervical cancer

  • Stage I: limited to the cervix; IA1 stromal invasion <3 mm in depth and <7 mm in horizontal extent; IA2 invasion 3-5 mm (extension <7 mm).
  • Stage IB: clinically visible lesion or invasion >5 mm; IB1 ≤4 cm; IB2 >4 cm.
  • Stage II: extension beyond the cervix but without reaching the pelvic wall; IIA without parametrium (IIA1 ≤4 cm, IIA2 >4 cm); IIB with parametrial invasion.
  • Stage III: extension to the pelvic wall or lower third of the vagina or hydronephrosis; IIIA lower third of vagina; IIIB pelvic wall/hydronephrosis; IIIC lymph node involvement (IIIC1 pelvic, IIIC2 para-aortic).
  • Stage IV: IVA invasion of the bladder or rectum; IVB distant metastasis.

items.title: Main symptoms
items.contentHtml:
Symptoms: postcoital bleeding and metrorrhagia

  • Postcoital bleeding is the most common symptom in early cervical cancer; It occurs due to friability of the tumor on contact.
  • Metrorrhagia (intermenstrual bleeding) and menorrhagia (heavy menstrual bleeding) are signs of advanced disease or endocervical injury.
  • Other late symptoms: watery or foul-smelling leukorrhea, pelvic pain, dysuria, rectal tenesmus and edema of the lower limbs due to obstruction lymphatic.
  • The differential diagnosis includes cervical polyps, infectious cervicitis (Chlamydia, Trichomonas), submucosal fibroids and endometritis; In the event of postcoital bleeding, cervical cytology and colposcopy should always be performed.

items.title: squamous intraepithelial lesion
items.contentHtml:
Squamous intraepithelial lesion

  • Low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (LSIL) corresponds to changes due to human papillomavirus (coilocytes) and mild dysplasia (CIN 1); high risk of spontaneous regression (>60% in 1 year).
  • High-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (HSIL) includes CIN 2 and CIN 3; risk of progression to invasive cancer of 30% in 10-20 years if untreated.
  • Management: LSIL in women <25 years is observed; HSIL requires conization (LEEP or diathermic loop) for diagnosis and treatment.
  • The histopathological report must specify whether the lesion compromises surgical margins; Positive margins increase the risk of persistence/recurrence.

items.title: Treatment by stage
items.contentHtml:
Treatment according to stage

  • Stage IA1: conization with free margins (LEEP or conization with cold scalpel) if fertility is desired; if not, simple hysterectomy.
  • Stage IA2: radical hysterectomy (type II) with pelvic lymphadenectomy; radical trachelectomy plus pelvic lymphadenectomy can be performed in patients with fertile desire.
  • Stage IB1 and IIA1: radical hysterectomy (type III) with pelvic lymphadenectomy; alternative: radiotherapy with concurrent chemotherapy (weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m²).
  • Stages IB2, IIA2, IIB-IVA: standard treatment is pelvic radiotherapy plus concurrent chemotherapy with cisplatin (40 mg/m² weekly for 5-6 weeks) and brachytherapy.
  • Stage IVB: palliative chemotherapy. (cisplatin/paclitaxel/bevacizumab).

items.title: Concurrent radiochemotherapy
items.contentHtml:
Concurrent radiotherapy and chemotherapy

  • Concurrent radiotherapy with chemotherapy (cisplatin 40 mg/m² weekly) is the standard for stages IB2-IVA; improves overall survival by 6-10% compared to radiotherapy alone.
  • External radiotherapy (45-50 Gy in 25-28 fractions) is combined with intracavitary brachytherapy (total dose 80-90 Gy to point A).
  • Acute adverse effects: actinic cystitis and proctitis, diarrhea, myelosuppression; late: vaginal stenosis, fistulas, pelvic fibrosis.
  • Concurrent chemotherapy is contraindicated in renal failure (creatinine clearance <30 ml/min) or severe peripheral neuropathy.

items.title: Risk factors
items.contentHtml:
Risk factors: immunosuppression and smoking

  • Smoking doubles the risk of cervical cancer; Smoke carcinogens concentrate in cervical mucus and damage cellular DNA, facilitating the persistence of human papillomavirus.
  • Immunosuppression (human immunodeficiency virus, transplant, chronic use of corticosteroids) increases the risk of persistent human papillomavirus infection and progression to high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion.
  • Women with human immunodeficiency virus should undergo cytology. cervical at diagnosis and then every 6-12 months if there is immunosuppression (CD4 <200 cells/µl).
  • Other factors: early initiation of sexual relations (<16 years), multiple sexual partners, multiparity and prolonged use of oral contraceptives (>5 years).

items.title: Screening
items.contentHtml:
Screening: start at age 25

  • Screening with cervical cytology (Pap smear) begins at 25 years; Before that age the incidence of cancer is very low and overtreatment of squamous intraepithelial lesions is common.
  • In women aged 25-29 years: cervical cytology every 3 years; in women aged 30-65 years: co-test (cytology + human papillomavirus test) every 5 years or cytology alone every 3 years.
  • In women >65 years of age with adequate prior screening (3 consecutive negative cytology tests or 2 negative co-tests in the last 10 years), screening can be suspended.
  • In women hysterectomized for a benign cause and without a history of injury high-grade squamous intraepithelial, no screening required.

items.title: Prevention
items.contentHtml:
Prevention: use of condom and vaccine against human papillomavirus

  • Use of condom reduces the transmission of human papillomavirus, but does not eliminate it completely, since the virus can infect areas not covered by the condom (vulva, perineum).
  • Vaccine against human papillomavirus is the main primary prevention strategy; It is recommended in both sexes before the beginning of sexual life (9-14 years).
  • Post-exposure vaccination is not effective to treat existing infections, but it prevents infection by other non-acquired genotypes.
  • Secondary prevention includes periodic screening with cervical cytology and human papillomavirus testing; Early detection reduces mortality by 60-80%.
